title: Unidentified Aerial Phenomena
subcommittee: Subcommittee on Counterterrorism, Counterintelligence, and Counterproliferation
summary: An open hearing followed by a closed classified briefing on the Department of Defense and Intelligence Community response to unidentified aerial phenomena, including reporting, stigma, flight safety, national security, data collection, analysis, and the creation of AOIMSG.
whyItMatters: This was the first open congressional hearing devoted to UAPs in roughly half a century. It publicly established UAP reporting, aviation safety, intelligence collection, stigma reduction, transparency, and executive-branch accountability as active congressional oversight issues.
editorialNote: GreyAlien separates the official hearing record from interpretation. Statements below summarize testimony by government officials and do not imply that every reported UAP case had an extraordinary origin.

witnesses.name: Ronald S. Moultrie
witnesses.roleAtHearing: Under Secretary of Defense for Intelligence and Security
witnesses.background: Senior Department of Defense intelligence official
witnesses.testimonySummary: Moultrie described the Department’s effort to create a standardized, data-driven process for collecting, analyzing, identifying, and mitigating UAP reports while balancing transparency with protection of sensitive sources and methods.
keyPoints: He described UAP as airborne objects that cannot be immediately identified.
He said the Department was establishing a dedicated office to coordinate collection, analysis, reporting, and mitigation.
He emphasized interagency collaboration and reducing the stigma surrounding reporting.

witnesses.name: Scott W. Bray
witnesses.testimonySummary: Bray described the UAP Task Force’s reporting and analysis work, increased reporting from military personnel, flight-safety and security concerns, sensor limitations, and efforts to move from anecdotal reports toward a rigorous scientific and technical process.
keyPoints: He testified that reports in military training areas were frequent and continuing.
He linked increased reporting to destigmatization, improved sensors, unmanned systems, clutter, and standardized procedures.
He said the Task Force sought a data-driven, science-and-engineering approach.

timeline.label: Hearing first published in the House Committee Repository
Open hearing held at 9:00 a.m. in HVC-210
Closed classified portion scheduled for noon in HVC-304
Transcript, member statements, and supporting materials added to the official record

documents.label: Rep. Gallagher submission for the record

evidenceProfile.summary: The hearing, transcript, member statements, and support documents are official records. The hearing included both open and classified portions.
